label: Scientific naturalism (contemporary philosophical)

scope.school: Contemporary ontological and methodological naturalism, including physicalism
limitations: Draft authored for schema validation (D1-002); sourced to encyclopedic surveys rather than primary works, and not yet domain-reviewed.
Naturalism is a family of positions, not a creed; this card records widely shared commitments, not the view of every self-described naturalist.
No claims are recorded for time, fundamental_problem, transformation, or destiny: the tradition carries no canonical doctrine on these primitives. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Reality is exhausted by nature and contains nothing supernatural; in slogan form, everything is physical.
claims.notes: What counts as 'physical' is itself debated within the school, but the grounding claim is shared across ontological naturalists.

claims.claim: There are no supernatural beings; all spatiotemporal entities are identical to or metaphysically constituted by physical entities.
The origins of the cosmos, life, and humanity involve no supernatural creation; whatever the detailed account, it is a matter for natural science.
claims.notes: Inferred from the ontological thesis plus methodological naturalism; the cited survey does not state a specific cosmogony, so confidence is medium.

claims.claim: The physical realm is causally closed: every event that has a cause has a physical cause; no non-physical agency intervenes in nature.
There is a single natural world, with no supernatural realms or ontologically distinct sacred domains.
Human beings are natural, physically constituted organisms continuous with the rest of nature.
Mental life supervenes on the physical and there is no immaterial soul; how consciousness is to be explained physically remains an open dispute within the school.
claims.notes: Contested aspect: whether and how consciousness reduces to or is realized by physical processes; there is no agreed-upon theory of consciousness (Van Gulick). The denial of an immaterial soul is not contested within the school.

claims.claim: The scientific method is the authoritative route to knowledge of reality and should be used to investigate all areas of it.

distinguishing_claims: No supernatural entities of any kind
Scientific method as the universal epistemic authority
common_confusions: Naturalism is not merely atheism: it is an ontological and methodological package, not only a denial of gods.
Methodological naturalism in scientific practice does not by itself entail ontological naturalism.
The absence of doctrines about transformation or destiny is not a doctrine of meaninglessness.

sources.title: Naturalism (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: David Papineau
sources.passage: reality is exhausted by nature, containing nothing 'supernatural' [...] all spatiotemporal entities must be identical to or metaphysically constituted by physical entities [...] the scientific method should be used to investigate all areas of reality. (First published 2007-02-22; substantive revision 2020-03-31.)

sources.title: Physicalism (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Daniel Stoljar
sources.passage: Physicalism is, in slogan form, the thesis that everything is physical. [...] no two possible worlds can be identical in their physical properties but differ, somewhere, in their mental, social or biological properties. Causal closure (sec. 6): every event possessing a cause has a physical cause. (First published 2001-02-13; substantive revision 2021-05-25.)

sources.title: Consciousness (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Robert Van Gulick
sources.passage: Despite the lack of any agreed upon theory of consciousness, there is a widespread, if less than universal, consensus that an adequate account of mind requires a clear understanding of it. (First published 2004-06-18; substantive revision 2014-01-14.)

review.notes: Drafted for schema validation (D1-002). All source URLs were fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Awaiting human domain review before any scholarly authority is claimed.
